preFix.title: Pole-lathe turned bobbin
preFix.subtitle: A green-wood sewing bobbin turned on the pole lathe
preFix.excerpt: A small sewing bobbin turned from green birch or apple on the pole lathe. The bobbin form introduces the parting cut, the cove, and the cylinder at the lathe. A useful first turnings project.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the pole-lathe turning method and bobbin form; Bealer The Tools That Built America (1976) for the green-wood turning tradition.

content.text: A sewing bobbin is a straightforward turning form: two flanges separated by a narrow waist. The green wood turns quickly on the pole lathe, which cuts on the downstroke of the foot pedal and returns with the spring pole on the upstroke. A bobbin 40 mm long and 30 mm at the flanges is a useful first turning because the form is short and clear, and the parting cuts that define the flanges are the key skill to learn.
Wood and lathe setup
Birch and apple are the recommendations: both turn cleanly from
green
and produce a smooth surface that does not need sanding before use as a bobbin. Start with a round
billet

60 mm long and 40 mm in diameter. Mount it between the lathe centres; the centres are the drive centre on the left (attached to the treadle-driven spindle) and the dead centre on the right (a fixed point the work turns against). Adjust the centres until the billet spins without wobble.

The cord runs from the treadle, around the billet, and up to the spring pole above. On the downstroke the cord winds the billet toward the operator; on the upstroke the spring pole lifts the cord and the billet reverses. The cutting tool is applied on the downstroke (the rotation toward the operator) only; on the upstroke the tool is lifted clear.
The turning tools

Pole-lathe turning uses three tools: a roughing gouge for bringing the billet from square to cylinder; a skew chisel or sloyd knife for the cylinder and parting cuts; and a small spindle gouge for the coves. On a first bobbin, the sloyd knife can substitute for dedicated lathe tools. Hold it on the tool rest with the blade horizontal, edge toward the work, and push the blade into the spinning billet.
Turning the cylinder

Press the treadle and bring the roughing gouge to the billet. The tool rest is set 10 mm from the surface of the billet. Lay the gouge on the rest, edge down, and advance it steadily into the spinning billet. A shaving lifts off the end of the billet and the cylinder begins to form. Keep cuts light; the pole lathe turns at lower speed than a powered lathe and heavy cuts stall the rotation. Work from the tailstock end (right) toward the headstock (left) in 10 mm passes.

The finished cylinder is 32 mm diameter. Check diameter with calipers; green birch and apple shrink slightly on the lathe face as they cut, so the finished wet diameter is 2 to 3 mm larger than the finished dry diameter.
Cutting the flanges

Mark the flange positions with pencil while the lathe is stationary: 10 mm in from each end for the outer flanges, and the waist between them at 18 to 22 mm from each end. Use the sloyd knife held square to the cylinder surface to make a parting cut at each flange shoulder. The knife is pushed straight in at 90 degrees to the long axis of the bobbin; each cut goes 5 mm deep to form the shoulder of the flange.
The waist between the flanges is reduced to 15 mm diameter by taking a series of angled cuts from each shoulder toward the centre. The
strop
the knife edge before the parting cuts; the shoulder cut is the hardest cut in the project and needs a keen edge.
Parting off

The bobbin is parted off at each end by pushing the knife straight in at the outermost flange shoulder lines until the bobbin separates from the supporting cylinder stubs. Hold the bobbin with a cloth as it parts; the spinning stub can flick the bobbin away.
Finishing

Sand the flange faces lightly with 320-grit to remove any saw marks from the parting cuts. Air-dry for one week. No oil finish is needed for a sewing bobbin; the dry wood is smooth enough for thread use.
Variations
Lace bobbin: 80 mm long, 10 mm flange diameter, 6 mm waist. The same form at a much smaller scale; requires a lighter tool touch.
Cotton reel form: a longer waist (30 mm) between shallower flanges for a traditional cotton reel.
Apple wood: denser than birch and turns to a finer surface; apple gives the cleanest finish of the easy-to-source green species.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Reduce the depth of cut to 1 to 2 mm per pass. The pole lathe's spring-pole system has limited torque at slow treadle speed; light frequent cuts are faster than single heavy ones.
attrs.cause: Cut depth too great for the pole-lathe torque
attrs.symptom: The lathe stalls on heavy cuts

attrs.fix: Strop the knife before parting cuts. Apply the knife only on the downstroke of the treadle; the upstroke reverses the spin and the knife will drag rather than cut.
attrs.cause: Knife dull or cutting on the upstroke
attrs.symptom: The parting cut leaves a rough shoulder

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
